definition: The ECB faces a structurally different energy transition policy problem than the Fed, creating persistent monetary policy divergence. CBAM adds input costs with no Fed analogue, LNG imports create ~$40-50B/yr structural USD demand, and EU ETS carbon prices at €65-80/t are policy-chosen cost increases the ECB must accommodate.
evidence: CBAM adding input costs to European production with no Fed analogue
LNG imports creating structural USD demand of ~$40-50B/yr
EU ETS carbon prices at €65-80/t functioning as persistent negative supply shock
Fed benefits from US energy independence, IRA fiscal subsidies, dollar reserve status
open_questions: Does the ECB explicitly accommodate carbon price inflation or treat it as a supply shock to look through?
How does Germany's debt brake interact with Green Deal fiscal ambitions?
Will the EUR-negative dynamics from LNG imports persist as European renewable capacity grows?
